items.str: 715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
Eighth District, Patrol — French Quarter Sector
Alley Rear of 712 Bourbon Street, New Orleans, LA 70116
Deceased — Pronounced at scene by EMS at 2:57 AM
On October 4, 2025, at approximately 2:40 AM, this officer received a dispatch call via PBPD Communications regarding an
unresponsive male discovered in the alley behind 712 Bourbon Street (The Rusty Anchor). The call originated from an
anonymous female caller who reported finding a male face-down and unresponsive in the alley. Caller was described as
panicked and distressed. EMS Unit 14 was simultaneously dispatched.
This officer arrived on scene at approximately 2:47 AM. Officer James Martin (Badge #3318) arrived as backup at
approximately 2:49 AM. The alley is accessed via a narrow passage off Bourbon Street running east behind a row of
establishments. The alley, approximately 12 feet wide, is poorly lit. One overhead security lamp was operational at the north
end; a second fixture at the south end was non-functional.
Upon entering the alley from the Bourbon Street approach, this officer observed the following:
1. A BLACK MALE, later identified as MARCUS WEBB (34), lying face-down approximately 28 feet from the mouth of the
alley. Victim was unresponsive, not breathing, and had no observable pulse. Significant blood pooling was present beneath
and around the victim's head and torso region.
2. Victim's clothing was heavily stained with blood, particularly at the upper back and collar area. Victim was wearing blue
jeans, a white t-shirt (heavily bloodstained), and white Nike low-top sneakers. Victim's clothing appeared disturbed but not
3. The victim's right cheek was resting against the asphalt. Visible contusions and lacerations were present on the right side
of the face. A partial shoe impression in blood was observed approximately 6 inches from the victim's ribcage area.
4. No weapon of any kind was observed in or around the victim's immediate area. Victim's wallet and cell phone were visible
— cell phone was face-down near victim's left hand. Wallet appeared intact.
5. The alley security camera (mounted on the rear exterior wall of The Rusty Anchor, approximately 14 feet high,
south-facing) was observed to be operational but pointed slightly east of the body's position. This officer noted the camera
as potentially relevant for footage review.
Upon arrival, this officer observed three individuals at varying distances from the scene:
A. An unidentified Black male (later identified as DARNELL WASHINGTON, 41) was standing in a doorway of an adjacent
establishment approximately 30 feet north. Mr. Washington was visibly shaken and indicated he had seen men in the alley
earlier. He was retained for witness interview.
B. THREE WHITE MALES were observed at the south end of the alley near the Bourbon Street intersection. Upon seeing
uniformed officers, the three individuals dispersed into the crowd on Bourbon Street before officers could detain them. This
officer radioed a description: three white males, one wearing a red short-sleeve shirt, one in a dark hoodie, one in a
light-colored button-down. Descriptions were broadcast to surrounding units. No immediate apprehension was made.
C. An anonymous Hispanic female (believed to be the 911 caller) was seen briefly at the alley entrance but departed before
officers could speak with her. She could not be located despite a subsequent canvas.
This officer established a crime scene perimeter with available barrier tape extending from the Bourbon Street entrance to
the rear wall of The Rusty Anchor. EMS Unit 14 (Paramedic Sandra Joubert) pronounced Marcus Webb deceased at 2:57
AM. EMS rendered no treatment due to obvious signs of death.
This officer notified the PBPD Homicide Division at 3:02 AM. Detective Renee Broussard (Badge #1887, Homicide Unit)
was assigned as lead investigator and notified at 3:05 AM. Crime Scene Technicians were requested and arrived at 3:41
The rear staff entrance of The Rusty Anchor was secured pending warrant. Bar owner/manager PHILLIP ARCENEAUX was
contacted at 3:18 AM and consented to investigators' presence inside the establishment for limited security camera review
Scene was preserved without contamination prior to Crime Scene Unit arrival. No items were moved or disturbed. Officer
Martin maintained outer perimeter. This officer maintained inner perimeter and primary witness contact.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
CSI Diane Tran, PBPD Crime Scene Unit #1104
Alley Rear of 712 Bourbon Street, New Orleans, LA 70116
Clear, 74°F, humidity 82%, no precipitation — dry alley surface
The crime scene is an alley running east-west behind the 700 block of Bourbon Street in the French Quarter, New Orleans.
The alley is approximately 12 feet wide and 95 feet long. The north wall consists of the rear facades of four commercial
establishments. The south wall is a masonry privacy barrier. The alley surface is aged asphalt with minor cracking —
surface was dry at time of examination.
Access points: (1) West end — gated entrance from Bourbon Street, gate was found open and unlocked. (2) East end —
connects to a secondary alley leading to Conti Street. (3) Staff door from The Rusty Anchor — a metal door located
approximately 32 feet from the Bourbon Street gate, on the north wall.
MARCUS WEBB was found face-down, oriented north-south, with head pointing north. The body was positioned
approximately 28 feet east of the Bourbon Street gate and approximately 6 feet south of the north alley wall. Right arm
extended, left arm partially beneath torso.
Visible injuries observed prior to autopsy removal: (1) Severe laceration at right temporal region with exposed subdermal
tissue. (2) Contusions and abrasions on both cheekbones. (3) Swelling and deformity consistent with nasal fracture. (4)
Lacerations and contusion at posterior skull — consistent with multiple blunt impacts. (5) Evidence of compression or
stomping injury: distinct linear impressions and contusion patterns on mid and lower back, visible through torn shirt material.
PRIMARY BLOOD POOL: Extending from beneath victim's head, approximately 3.5 feet diameter. Consistent with fatal
head wound. Blood was partially coagulated at time of discovery, consistent with death occurring 30–90 minutes prior to
SECONDARY BLOOD PATTERN: Scattered droplets (medium velocity spatter) beginning approximately 8 feet west of
victim's position, creating a trail indicating the victim may have been mobile or struck while upright, then moved or fell
SHOE IMPRESSION IN BLOOD: One clear partial shoe impression (outsole pattern) observed in the blood pool,
approximately 6 inches from victim's right ribcage. Impression swabbed and photographed (Photo Log Items 19–22).
Impression cast attempted — surface porosity reduced cast quality but pattern was documented photographically. Pattern
appeared consistent with athletic shoe outsole.
North wall, 11 ft east of staff door
NO WEAPON RECOVERED: Despite thorough search of alley, adjacent areas, nearby dumpsters, and drainage grates, no
blunt instrument or weapon of any kind was recovered. The force and pattern of injuries suggest a weapon of substantial
weight or repeated manual strikes and stomping. The absence of a weapon indicates it was removed from the scene.
NOT A ROBBERY: Victim's wallet contained $340.00 in cash (three $100 bills, one $40.00). Victim's iPhone 13 was
present. No signs of a struggle over personal property. Conclusion: robbery was not the motive.
The alley surveillance camera (south-facing, mounted on The Rusty Anchor exterior) was examined. Bar owner Phillip
Arceneaux confirmed the system records to an internal DVR. Upon initial review, a gap in alley footage was noted from
approximately 1:15 AM to 1:37 AM — a period of 22 minutes. Footage resumes at 1:37 AM showing the victim already on
the ground. Digital Forensics was notified to examine the DVR hardware. (See Digital Forensics Report, Document 25.)

CSI Diane Tran, PBPD Crime Scene Unit #1104
Alley Rear of 712 Bourbon Street, New Orleans, LA 70116
Approximate — not to scale. See measurements below.
East along alley, north wall side
The alley camera covers approximately 40 feet of alley from the camera mount eastward toward Conti Street. The body was
located approximately 28 feet from the Bourbon Street gate — WITHIN the camera's field of view. The 22-minute gap in
footage (1:15 AM – 1:37 AM) therefore obscures the critical period during which the assault occurred. The staff door
(through which victim and suspects exited) is at the near western edge of camera coverage.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
The undersigned officer, Detective Renee Broussard, Badge #1887, PBPD Homicide Division, being duly sworn, deposes
and states that she has probable cause to believe that evidence of the crime of Second-Degree Murder (La. R.S. 14:30.1) is
located at the places and in the items described herein.
I. ITEMS TO BE SEARCHED AND SEIZED
1. DEREK FONTENOT, DOB 07/12/1997, 2847 Magazine Street, Apt. 3B, New Orleans, LA 70115 — cellular telephone(s)
and all electronic devices capable of storing communications.
2. TRAVIS BELL, DOB 03/08/1994, 1104 St. Claude Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70117 — cellular telephone(s) and all
electronic devices capable of storing communications.
3. RYAN McCALL, DOB 11/22/1995, 5533 Magazine Street, Apt. 1, New Orleans, LA 70115 — cellular telephone(s) and all
electronic devices capable of storing communications.
1. 2019 Chevrolet Malibu, Silver, Louisiana License Plate ZBK-4419, registered to RYAN McCALL. Vehicle located at 534
Conti Street at time of initial stop at 3:00 AM October 4, 2025. Vehicle to be searched for biological evidence, weapons, and
2. 2021 Toyota Camry, Black, Louisiana License Plate XRD-7721, registered to DEREK FONTENOT. Parked at or near
1. 2847 Magazine Street, Apt. 3B, New Orleans, LA 70115 — Residence of Derek Fontenot. Search for: clothing worn on
evening of October 3–4, 2025; footwear consistent with shoe impression at scene; biological evidence; weapons or blunt
2. 1104 St. Claude Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70117 — Residence of Travis Bell. Search for: clothing worn on evening of
October 3–4, 2025; biological evidence; any items with blood transfer.
3. 5533 Magazine Street, Apt. 1, New Orleans, LA 70115 — Residence of Ryan McCall. Search for: clothing worn on
On October 4, 2025, at approximately 2:40 AM, PBPD officers responded to a homicide at the alley behind 712 Bourbon
Street. Victim MARCUS WEBB, 34, was found deceased from blunt force trauma. Investigation revealed the following
(1) Bar surveillance footage shows DEREK FONTENOT, TRAVIS BELL, and RYAN McCALL engaged in a heated
altercation with the victim inside The Rusty Anchor approximately 75 minutes before victim's death. Bartender witness
states the altercation was physical in nature and that at least one suspect made threatening statements toward the victim.
(2) Bar surveillance footage shows all three suspects exiting through the rear staff door at 1:18 AM — three minutes after
the victim exited through the same door at 1:15 AM.
(3) A witness (DARNELL WASHINGTON) observed three males matching the suspects' general descriptions entering the
alley from the staff door area at approximately 1:20 AM.
(4) DEREK FONTENOT's left sneaker bears blood consistent with blood type A+, matching the victim's blood type. TRAVIS
BELL has fresh lacerations on his right knuckles consistent with striking another person.
(5) RYAN McCALL was located sitting in his vehicle at 534 Conti Street at 3:00 AM and claims to have been in his vehicle
since 1 AM. Initial cell tower data analysis places his phone in the area immediately adjacent to the alley at 1:28 AM,
UPON consideration of the foregoing affidavit, the Court finds that probable cause exists to issue this Search Warrant. IT IS
HEREBY ORDERED that any PBPD officer may search the described persons, vehicles, and residences and seize all items
described herein. This warrant is valid for execution within ten (10) days of issuance. Louisiana Code of Criminal Procedure
Hon. Judge Patricia Fonville, Section F, Criminal District Court
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
Eighth District, Patrol — French Quarter Sector
Det. Renee Broussard, Badge #1887, Homicide Division
I am Officer James Martin, Badge 3318, Eighth District. I'm providing this statement regarding the homicide call at 712
I was two blocks south on Bourbon when Officer Thibodaux arrived at the scene. I arrived at approximately 2:49 AM.
Thibodaux had already established an inner perimeter and was speaking with a Black male in a doorway — later IDed as
Darnell Washington. She briefed me in about thirty seconds and asked me to take the outer perimeter.
I managed the outer perimeter at the alley entrance on Bourbon Street. A small crowd had gathered. The French Quarter on
a Friday night means there's always people around, especially at that hour. People are stumbling out of bars, most are
intoxicated. No one approached me claiming to have witnessed anything. I asked several bystanders — most said they had
Thibodaux had radioed about three white males who fled. I did not personally see those individuals. By the time I arrived,
the described individuals were not visible. I queried two or three bystanders about whether they'd seen three men come out
of the alley — one woman said she might have but couldn't be sure. She was intoxicated and I did not take a full statement.
I maintained perimeter until Crime Scene arrived at 3:41 AM. EMS Unit 14 had arrived before me. The paramedic — I
believe her name was Sandra Joubert — pronounced the victim at 2:57 AM. She and her partner did not touch anything
after confirming death and waited for instructions.
At approximately 3:00 AM I was notified via radio that a Ryan McCall had been found sitting in a silver Malibu on Conti
Street, two blocks over. The unit that found him noted he appeared nervous and had what looked like minor scuff marks on
his forearm. He was detained for Detective Broussard at approximately 3:05 AM. I did not make direct contact with him.
My role was scene security. I have no additional information beyond what is documented. Everything I observed is
consistent with what Officer Thibodaux reported.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
ACTIVE — No arrests as of final entry date
Got the call from Homicide dispatch. On scene by 3:20 AM. Thibodaux did a solid job holding the perimeter. Victim is
face-down in the alley, significant blood loss, no weapon. CSU requested. The alley stinks of stale beer and garbage — this
is two hundred feet from Bourbon Street on a Friday night. Anyone could have walked through here. The camera is the key.
Need to pull that footage tonight.
Spoke with Darnell Washington in the alley. He's a night owl — says he comes out here to smoke most nights because his
landlord doesn't allow it inside. He saw three men enter the alley from the staff door around 1:20 AM. Can't give me faces —
it was dark and he was thirty feet away. He says one had a red shirt. He heard 'raised voices and some kind of scuffle' but
not words. He went inside. That's all he's got. But it's something.
Phillip Arceneaux (bar owner) let me look at the indoor DVR. Gold. The indoor camera shows a heated argument at the bar
— three white men squaring up on a Black male. Bartender (young blonde woman — need her ID) gets in between. At 1:15
AM, the Black male (victim) goes out the back. At 1:18 AM, all three white males go out the same door. Three minutes apart.
Then the alley camera goes dark. 22 MINUTES. When it comes back at 1:37 AM, Marcus Webb is already on the ground.
CAMERA GAP: Arceneaux says the alley cam 'malfunctions sometimes.' I don't buy it. 22 minutes of darkness, starts
exactly when the suspects enter the alley, ends when the victim is already down and they've cleared out? That is not a
malfunction. That is deletion or interference. Flagging Digital Forensics for full DVR examination.
Ryan McCall was found in his car two blocks over at 3 AM. He says he left the bar at 1 AM. The indoor camera has him
leaving through the BACK DOOR at 1:18 AM with the other two. He's lying. Cell tower data will tell the story — put in the
Search warrant signed by Judge Fonville. Executing warrants today. Derek Fontenot's Nike Air Force 1 (left foot) — blood
on the sole. Lab needs to type it. If it's A+, that matches Marcus Webb. Fontenot says he stepped in a puddle on Bourbon.
Bourbon Street has a lot of things on the ground at 2 AM. Blood from a beating victim is not typically one of them.
Travis Bell — fresh laceration, right knuckle area, over the index and middle finger. He says he fell. The laceration pattern is
consistent with striking a hard surface or a face. The bruising pattern around it is not consistent with a fall. These are fighting
knuckles. Getting the medical examiner to look at the photos.
Autopsy with Dr. Guidry today. Preliminary findings: multiple blunt force impacts to head and face, rib fractures (3 ribs —
bilateral), boot/shoe impression pattern on torso consistent with stomping. TOD estimated 1:30–2:15 AM. BAC 0.14 —
Marcus was drunk but functional. They didn't catch him passed out. They went after him.
DNA prelim back. Epithelial cells on Marcus's shirt collar consistent with Derek Fontenot — someone grabbed him by the
collar. DNA under his fingernails — unknown male, cannot exclude Travis Bell. Marcus fought back. Good for him. Not
Cell tower data confirmed: Ryan McCall's phone pinged a tower covering the alley area at 1:28 AM. He was NOT in his car
at 1 AM like he says. He was in that alley or right next to it when Marcus Webb was being beaten to death. Also — found a
call log item: Derek called Ryan at 1:14 AM. That's the rally call. 'Come on, let's go.' One minute later, Derek calls Travis.
Four minutes later, all three walk out the back door. This was coordinated.
Text message recovered from Travis Bell's phone: sent to Derek at 2:48 AM on October 4th — 'Don't say anything.' Six
minutes after a 911 call reported Marcus's body. Travis KNEW. They all knew.
Re-interviewed bartender Shannon O'Brien. Her first statement only mentioned Derek Fontenot. She was holding back.
Today she told me all three were involved in the altercation. And she told me Travis Bell said — verbatim — 'we'll finish this
outside.' That is a direct verbal threat, premeditation, seven minutes before they killed Marcus Webb. Why didn't she tell me
this first? She knows these guys. The Rusty Anchor is their regular bar.
Forensic shoe analysis: Derek's Nike AF1, sole pattern matches the partial shoe impression in blood at the scene.
MATCHES. The blood on his shoe is A+, matching the victim. I have him at the scene. I have him stomping Marcus Webb.
Anonymous tip via tip line: caller says Derek Fontenot was 'bragging about what happened' at a bar called Patout's on
Magazine Street on Saturday afternoon — the day after the murder. Need to canvass Patout's, pull their cameras, find the
witnesses. This could be the witness that puts the nail in.
Status: All three suspects have retained counsel. No confessions. Building circumstantial case. Evidence: (1) motive —
altercation over Derek's ex; (2) opportunity — all three on scene per camera and cell data; (3) physical evidence — Derek's
blood-soaked shoe with scene-matching sole, DNA, Travis's fighting knuckles; (4) Travis's 'don't say anything' text; (5)
Ryan's contradicted alibi; (6) Shannon's second statement with verbal threat. The 22-minute camera gap is the biggest hole
in the case. Need to prove it was intentional. Digital Forensics working on it. Preparing case summary for DA presentation.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
PBPD Eighth District Station, Interview Room 1
This supplemental report documents a second interview with witness Shannon O'Brien, who was previously interviewed on
October 4, 2025 (see Document 11). O'Brien's initial statement was limited in scope and identified only one suspect —
DEREK FONTENOT — as being involved in the altercation with the victim. This detective found the initial statement
materially incomplete and requested a follow-up interview after reviewing the bar's indoor surveillance footage, which clearly
shows three men involved in the altercation.
When confronted with the indoor surveillance footage in this second interview, Ms. O'Brien became visibly distressed. After
a period of approximately five minutes during which she stated she 'didn't want to get anyone in trouble,' she provided the
1. ALL THREE MEN INVOLVED: O'Brien confirmed that DEREK FONTENOT, TRAVIS BELL, and RYAN McCALL were all
involved in the altercation with Marcus Webb. In her initial statement, O'Brien stated only that 'Derek and Marcus got into it.'
She now acknowledges all three men were 'in Marcus's face' during the altercation.
2. NATURE OF ALTERCATION: O'Brien states the altercation began when Derek Fontenot became aware that his
ex-girlfriend — who was at the bar earlier in the evening — had been speaking and dancing with Marcus Webb. O'Brien
describes Derek as 'furious' and states that Travis Bell and Ryan McCall were 'hyping Derek up.'
3. KEY VERBAL THREAT — TRAVIS BELL: O'Brien states that TRAVIS BELL made the following statement during the
altercation: 'We'll finish this outside.' O'Brien is certain of this statement. She states she heard it clearly because she was
standing directly behind the bar, approximately six feet from the three men. She did not include this in her initial statement
because she 'didn't think it meant anything' and 'didn't want trouble.'
4. WHY SHE HELD BACK: O'Brien states that Derek Fontenot is a 'regular' at The Rusty Anchor and that she knows him
'somewhat.' She was worried about 'making enemies' in the neighborhood. She denies being asked or pressured by anyone
Travis Bell's statement — 'We'll finish this outside' — constitutes direct evidence of premeditation and a verbal threat made
approximately 75 minutes before the victim's estimated time of death. The statement was made while all three suspects
were confronting the victim inside the bar. The victim exited through the back door at 1:15 AM. All three suspects followed
through the same door at 1:18 AM. This timeline, combined with Travis Bell's explicit verbal threat, substantially strengthens
the inference that the subsequent assault was planned.
O'Brien's credibility is bolstered by the indoor surveillance footage, which is consistent with her revised account. Her initial
omission of Travis Bell and Ryan McCall, while troubling, appears motivated by social reluctance rather than malicious
intent. This detective notes that O'Brien became more forthcoming once shown the footage, and her revised statement is
internally consistent and corroborated by video evidence.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
WITNESS CANVASS REPORT — BOURBON STREET AREA
Det. Renee Broussard #1887 / Officer Maria Thibodaux #2241
700 block Bourbon Street, adjacent alleys, Conti Street block
22 individuals; 8 provided statements of varying value
Officers conducted door-to-door canvass of all commercial establishments on the 700 block of Bourbon Street and adjacent
properties. Businesses contacted: The Rusty Anchor (712), Magnolia Spirits Bar (714), Voodoo Lounge (708), Creole
Kitchen (706), Bayou Gifts (710), and four residential/mixed-use buildings on Conti and Bienville Streets. Additionally,
officers spoke with street vendors and night workers in the area.
DARNELL WASHINGTON, M/B, 41, residing at 718 Bourbon Street, Apt. 4 (above Magnolia Spirits). Mr. Washington was
identified at the scene by Officer Thibodaux and formally interviewed during the canvass. Mr. Washington is a regular
night-shift worker (riverboat tour operator, days off that night) who states he comes to the alley doorway most nights to
KEY STATEMENT: Washington states that at approximately 1:20 AM he observed three males exit through the staff door of
The Rusty Anchor into the alley. He described them as 'white guys — three of them.' He states one was wearing a red shirt
and one appeared to be in a dark jacket or hoodie. He could not provide facial descriptions — the alley is poorly lit and he
was approximately 30 feet away. He states the men appeared 'amped up' and were moving quickly. He did not hear
conversation clearly but noted raised voices.
Washington states he went back inside his building approximately five minutes later and heard 'some kind of commotion in
the alley' — described as 'like something hitting the ground.' He did not look out. He heard nothing after that. He did not call
NOTE: Travis Bell was confirmed by indoor bar surveillance to be wearing a RED SHORT-SLEEVE SHIRT on the night of
October 3–4, 2025. Washington's description of a man in a red shirt is consistent with Travis Bell.
VOODOO LOUNGE STAFF (708 Bourbon): Door staff (male, Hispanic, would not give name) stated he saw 'a group of
white guys running from the alley direction' at approximately 1:30 AM. Could not describe further. Did not want to make
CREOLE KITCHEN EMPLOYEE (706 Bourbon): Night cleaning employee stated she heard 'yelling and loud banging' from
the alley direction at approximately 1:25–1:30 AM. She looked out the rear window but could not see clearly due to
STREET VENDOR (700 block, cart near alley entrance): Male, age approx. 50s, stated he packs up at 1:00 AM. Was gone
before the incident. No relevant information.
Fourteen additional contacts were made with bar patrons, pedestrians, and business staff. None provided information
relevant to the period between 1:15 AM and 2:40 AM in or near the alley. The French Quarter's ambient noise level and
pedestrian volume make witness identification difficult. Several contacts were intoxicated and unreliable.
The female caller who made the 911 call at 2:40 AM was not identified during the canvass. Despite officers' presence on
Bourbon Street throughout the morning, no individual came forward. Call records indicate the call was made from an iPhone
registered to an unrelated subscriber — the phone may have been borrowed or the subscriber may not wish to be identified.
Investigation into the caller's identity is ongoing.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
Bartender, The Rusty Anchor, 712 Bourbon Street
I've been bartending at The Rusty Anchor for about two years. I was working the closing shift last night — I came on at 6 PM
and was supposed to close up at 3 AM.
We were busy. Friday nights on Bourbon are always crazy. Around midnight, maybe a little after, I noticed a situation at the
far end of the bar. There was a Black man — I didn't know him, he wasn't a regular — and he had been talking with a young
woman most of the night. The woman is Derek's ex. Derek Fontenot. He comes in a lot.
Derek got upset when he saw her with this other man. He walked over and things got heated. They were arguing — Derek
and the other man. I couldn't hear everything, music was loud. I went over and told them to cool it or take it outside. It
seemed to settle down after that.
A little while later — maybe around 1:15 or so — the man I didn't know left through the back. I don't know why he used the
back door. Maybe he knew where it was. Maybe someone told him. I don't know.
After that the night sort of moved on. I started closing prep. I didn't see anything else unusual until the police showed up.
That's when I found out something had happened in the alley.
The man arguing with Marcus — that's Derek Fontenot. He's a regular. He tips okay. I've never seen him get violent but he
NOTE: This statement was given voluntarily. Witness identified the incident as an altercation between the victim and DEREK FONTENOT
only. No mention of additional individuals involved. Detective Broussard noted witness appeared nervous and guarded during interview.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
PBPD Eighth District Station, Interview Room 1
Inconsistency with surveillance footage — initial statement incomplete
Detective Broussard: Shannon, thank you for coming in. I want to show you something. [Surveillance footage shown —
indoor camera, approximately 12:05 AM, showing three men at the bar with the victim]
Shannon O'Brien: [long pause] I know. I know there were three of them. I'm sorry I didn't say that the first time. I was scared.
These guys — Derek especially — they come in all the time. I have to work there.
Detective Broussard: Tell me about all three men.
Shannon O'Brien: It was Derek Fontenot, Travis Bell, and Ryan McCall. They all come in together sometimes. Travis and
Ryan are friends of Derek's. That night, all three of them went up to this man — Marcus, I know his name now — and Derek
was real angry. He was saying things like 'stay away from her' and calling Marcus names. Travis was right there backing
Derek up. Ryan was kind of standing to the side but he wasn't leaving.
Detective Broussard: Did any of them make specific threats?
Shannon O'Brien: Travis. Travis Bell said — I remember this clearly because it made me nervous — he said, 'We'll finish
this outside.' He was looking right at Marcus when he said it. Marcus didn't respond, he just kind of nodded and backed off. I
thought it was just trash talk. I thought Marcus was going to leave and that would be the end of it.
Detective Broussard: Did Marcus leave after that?
Shannon O'Brien: Yeah. Maybe twenty, thirty minutes later. He went out the back. The staff door — people sometimes use it
as a shortcut. I was surprised he knew it was there, but maybe he'd used it before or saw a sign.
Detective Broussard: And the three men — Derek, Travis, Ryan — what did they do?
Shannon O'Brien: [long pause] They left not long after. I thought they were just leaving for the night. I didn't — I didn't think
they were going to actually do anything. I really didn't. When I found out in the morning what happened, I was sick. I felt sick.
Detective Broussard: Is there anything else you held back in your first statement?
Shannon O'Brien: No. That's everything. I swear. I just — I was scared. Derek knows people in the neighborhood. I didn't
DETECTIVE NOTE: Witness's revised account is fully corroborated by indoor surveillance footage. Travis Bell's statement 'We'll finish this
outside' constitutes a direct verbal threat and evidence of premeditation. Witness was credible in this second interview. Initial omission
attributed to fear of retaliation from known bar regulars.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
Riverboat Tour Operator, New Orleans Steamboat Company
718 Bourbon Street, Apt. 4, New Orleans, LA 70116
PBPD Eighth District Station, Interview Room 3
I live above Magnolia Spirits at 718 Bourbon. Been there six years. I work the riverboats, day shifts mostly, so I'm up late
most nights. My landlord's got a no-smoking policy in the building, which I respect but don't enjoy, so I go down to the alley
door on the side to smoke most nights. It's a habit. Been doing it for years.
Last night — Friday night going into Saturday — I went down around 1 AM, maybe a little after. The alley was quiet when I
first got there. I was leaning in the doorway, smoking. You can see down the alley pretty good from that doorway, maybe
Around — I'd say 1:20, give or take — I heard a door bang open. The staff door for The Rusty Anchor. It's metal, makes a
hell of a sound when it swings hard. Three men came out. They were white guys. Moving fast, moving with purpose — not
like they were leaving, more like they were going somewhere specific. You know the difference. I've lived on Bourbon Street
I couldn't see their faces good. The light in that alley is terrible — there's one bulb at the far end near Bourbon Street and
that's about it. The area around the staff door is dark. I could see their general shapes and colors. One of them had on a red
shirt — short sleeve. One had something darker on top, like a jacket maybe. Third one I couldn't make out.
They went east down the alley, away from me. They were talking but not loud enough for me to hear words. After a minute
or two I heard raised voices. Then I heard something — like a heavy sound, thudding. Couldn't tell you exactly what it was.
Could've been a lot of things. On Bourbon Street late on a Friday you hear all kinds.
I finished my cigarette and went back inside. I didn't think much of it. About an hour later there were sirens and I looked out
my window and saw the police lights. That's when I went back down.
Detective Broussard: Can you identify any of the three men?
Darnell Washington: I want to be honest with you — no. I can't make a definitive ID. It was dark and I wasn't paying real
close attention. Three white men, one red shirt, one darker top. That's what I've got. I don't want to say more than I actually
Detective Broussard: Could you estimate age or build?
Darnell Washington: Young. Late twenties, early thirties. Average builds — none of them was particularly big. The one in the
red shirt might've been the biggest of the three but I'm guessing at that point.

Canvass follow-up — Reyes contacted PBPD after seeing news report
I saw the news about the guy killed in the alley behind The Rusty Anchor. I was there that night. I was with a friend —
coworker, actually, named Pete Guidroz — we were there from about 10:30 until almost midnight. I wasn't going to say
anything at first, but it's been bugging me. I figured I should come in.
Around midnight — maybe 12:05, 12:10 — I noticed a commotion at the end of the bar. There was a Black man, tall,
wearing a white t-shirt. Three white guys were getting in his face. One of them was really going at it — pointing, leaning in,
you could tell he was furious. The other two were right there with him. It wasn't just one guy — all three were involved.
The bartender — young blonde girl — came over and got between them. That helped for a minute. But the one in the red
shirt — Travis, I heard someone call him Travis — he was still saying stuff after she walked away. Angry stuff. I wasn't close
enough to hear every word but his body language was threatening. He kept looking at the Black guy like he wanted to go.
The other two — Derek and I think the other one was Ryan, I heard those names too — Derek was the most verbal. He
seemed the most personally upset. Ryan was quieter but he wasn't backing anyone down. He was standing right there, part
The situation died down and my friend and I left about fifteen minutes later. I did not see what happened after that. When I
heard on the news that a man was killed in the alley behind the same bar, same night — I knew those were the same guys.
Detective Broussard: How confident are you that Travis Bell was aggressive?
Carlos Reyes: Very confident. The other two were mad but Travis was the one who seemed like he might actually do
something. He had that look. You work in restaurants long enough, you see fights. He had that look.

PBPD Eighth District Station, Interview Room 3
Marcus Webb was my best friend. We grew up together in Baton Rouge. He moved to New Orleans about six months ago
— April of this year. He got a job offer from a logistics company in Metairie. It was a good opportunity. He was excited about
The night he died — that was supposed to be a celebration. He had just gotten official word that his job offer was finalized,
his salary confirmed. He texted me that afternoon: 'going out tonight to celebrate, wish you were here.' I was in Baton
Marcus didn't have enemies. That's not a cliche — I mean it literally. He was the kind of person who got along with
everyone. He was funny, he was generous, he worked hard. He had no criminal record, no debts that I know of. He didn't do
He didn't know anyone in New Orleans well enough to have enemies there. He'd been there six months. He knew me, a few
coworkers, his landlord. He wasn't in any kind of trouble.
I've been told about the altercation at the bar — some man whose girlfriend or ex talked to Marcus. That's so Marcus. He'd
talk to anyone. He'd never mean anything by it. He wasn't a confrontational person. He would've backed down from a fight
— he always did. He would've walked away. He wasn't stupid. He knew when to leave.
Detective Broussard: Did Marcus have any prior conflicts with anyone in New Orleans?
Jerome Harris: None. Absolutely none. He'd been there six months and he barely knew anyone. He didn't deserve this. He
was thirty-four years old with a new job and a whole life ahead of him. He moved to New Orleans to start fresh. He was just
Detective Broussard: Do you know of any insurance policies, financial arrangements, or anyone who might benefit from
Jerome Harris: His mother is his beneficiary on his life insurance — a small policy he had from work. His mother is Evelyn
Webb, she's in Baton Rouge. She's devastated. Marcus had no enemies. This was random violence. Or it was because
these men decided one conversation in a bar was worth killing someone over.

ORLEANS PARISH OFFICE OF THE MEDICAL EXAMINER
Dr. Thomas R. Guidry, MD — Chief Medical Examiner, Orleans Parish
Multiple blunt force injuries to head and torso
The body is that of a well-developed, well-nourished Black male appearing consistent with the stated age of 34 years.
Height: 6'1". Weight: 192 lbs. The decedent was received clothed in a white cotton t-shirt (heavily bloodstained), blue denim
jeans, white Nike low-top sneakers, and black socks. Clothing was examined and retained as evidence.
1. LACERATION, right temporal region, approximately 3.4 cm in length, with underlying subgaleal hemorrhage and fracture
of the right temporal bone. Laceration margins are irregular, consistent with impact against a hard surface or struck with a
blunt instrument. Significant blood loss from this wound.
2. LACERATION, posterior skull, approximately 2.1 cm, with underlying occipital fracture. Pattern consistent with at least
two separate blunt impacts to the posterior cranium.
3. CONTUSIONS, bilateral cheekbones. Multiple abrasions on chin consistent with contact with asphalt surface.
4. NASAL FRACTURE — comminuted fracture of the nasal bones. Hemorrhagic tissue throughout nasal passages.
Consistent with a direct blunt impact.
5. Multiple contusions across mid and lower back, ranging from 4 cm to 9 cm diameter. The pattern and distribution are
CONSISTENT WITH STOMPING or repeated stamping with a shod foot. At least three distinct impression-pattern
contusions were identified, suggesting a minimum of three separate stomping impacts.
6. LINEAR IMPRESSION CONTUSIONS: Two parallel linear marks, approximately 1.2 cm wide, spaced approximately 2.8
cm apart, located at the right mid-flank. Consistent with the tread pattern of an athletic shoe outsole.
7. Defensive wounds: abrasions on the dorsum of both forearms, consistent with defensive posturing. Contusion on left
palm. These injuries indicate Marcus Webb was conscious and attempting to protect himself during at least part of the
CRANIAL: Bilateral subdural hematoma (right greater than left). Right temporal bone fracture with epidural hematoma. Left
parietal contusion. Diffuse subarachnoid hemorrhage. These injuries are consistent with multiple separate blunt impacts and
are consistent with the pattern of being struck and/or having the head contact the ground.
THORAX: Right side: fractures of ribs 5, 6, and 7 at the mid-axillary line. Left side: fractures of ribs 8 and 9. These rib
fractures are consistent with both direct striking blows and stomping impacts. No pneumothorax. Pulmonary contusion, right
III. CAUSE AND MANNER OF DEATH
Multiple blunt force injuries to the head and torso, including bilateral subdural hematoma, skull
Blunt force stomping injuries to the torso.
Based on body temperature (84.2°F rectal at 4:30 AM), rigor mortis (developing, not complete), and liver mortis (fixed
posteriorly, blanching in upper areas at scene), estimated time of death is between 1:30 AM and 2:15 AM on October 4,
2025. This is consistent with investigative information placing the assault between 1:15 AM and 1:40 AM.

Dr. Angela Trosclair, PhD — Forensic Toxicologist
The decedent had a blood alcohol concentration of 0.14 g/dL at the time of death. This level is consistent with moderate
intoxication. At this concentration, an individual of Mr. Webb's size (192 lbs) would be expected to experience impaired
coordination and reaction time, but would remain conscious, ambulatory, and capable of defensive actions. The BAC of 0.14
The vitreous humor ethanol level of 0.16 g/dL is slightly higher than the femoral blood level, which is consistent with the
normal post-mortem redistribution pattern and confirms that alcohol was consumed before death.
No controlled substances, illicit drugs, or prescription medications were detected. This is not a drug-facilitated assault case.
The decedent's impairment was limited to alcohol at a level consistent with voluntary social consumption.

Forensic Examiner Patrice Delacroix — PBPD Forensic Services Unit
SE-001: Left Nike Air Force 1 Low sneaker — white/white, size 11 US
Recovered from DEREK FONTENOT during search warrant execution, October 4, 2025
Crime scene shoe impression — Item CS-003
SE-001 is a left-foot Nike Air Force 1 Low sneaker, white colorway with white sole, men's size 11 US. The shoe is in used
condition with moderate wear to the outsole at the heel and lateral forefoot. The shoe was received in a paper evidence bag,
sealed and initialed by Detective Broussard and CSI Tran.
Visual examination of the outsole revealed a dark reddish-brown staining pattern on the heel and mid-sole region of the left
shoe. Field presumptive testing (Modified Kastle-Meyer) returned POSITIVE for blood. The staining is concentrated at the
heel strike zone and extends across approximately 40% of the outsole surface.
BLOOD TYPING: The blood on SE-001 was typed using ABO/Rh immunological testing. Result: BLOOD TYPE A
POSITIVE (A+). The victim Marcus Webb's blood type was A+ (confirmed by Orleans Parish Medical Examiner and victim
medical records). Derek Fontenot's blood type is O negative (O-), as confirmed by his medical records obtained under
search warrant. The blood on Fontenot's shoe IS NOT HIS OWN BLOOD.
The outsole pattern of SE-001 was compared to Crime Scene Item CS-003 — the partial shoe impression in blood
photographed at the scene (Photo Log items 12–14). The Nike Air Force 1 Low outsole features a distinctive
herringbone-pattern tread with concentric circle medallion pattern at the heel and forefoot pivot point.
COMPARISON RESULT: The partial impression from the crime scene IS CONSISTENT WITH the outsole pattern of
SE-001 in all comparable features. The following pattern elements are in correspondence: (1) herringbone tread orientation
at mid-sole; (2) heel medallion partial pattern; (3) outsole width measurement (approximately 10.5 cm at widest point). No
features in the crime scene impression are INCONSISTENT with SE-001.
LIMITATION: The crime scene impression is partial — approximately 60% of the full outsole area is represented. Full
individualizing comparison is not possible. However, the examiner finds the pattern correspondence sufficient to conclude
that SE-001 CANNOT BE EXCLUDED as the source of the crime scene impression.
1. SE-001 (Derek Fontenot's left Nike Air Force 1) bears blood of Type A+, consistent with and not excluding victim Marcus
Webb as the source. The blood is not consistent with the shoe's owner (Fontenot, blood type O-).
2. The outsole pattern of SE-001 is consistent with the partial shoe impression recovered from the blood pool adjacent to the
3. The combination of A+ blood on the outsole and outsole pattern consistency with the crime scene impression supports
the conclusion that SE-001 was present at the crime scene and was in contact with the victim's blood.
Capt. Robert Hebert, PBPD Forensic Services Unit

Victim blood type, not Bell's (Bell O+)
All blood identified at the crime scene and on suspect evidence items is BLOOD TYPE A POSITIVE (A+). This is consistent
with the victim Marcus Webb's blood type (A+). No blood of any other type was detected at the scene or on any evidence
items. The presence of A+ blood on Derek Fontenot's left sneaker and on Travis Bell's right knuckle laceration area is
particularly significant, as neither Fontenot (O-) nor Bell (O+) has A+ blood.
NOTE: ABO blood typing is a presumptive serological test. The findings are consistent with victim blood but do not individually identify the
source with the same certainty as DNA analysis. DNA analysis has been separately requested for all items (see DNA Analysis Report, Doc
21). Blood typing results are consistent with and supportive of the DNA findings.

Digital Forensic Examiner Brandon St. Amant, PBPD DFU
Ryan McCall's Samsung Galaxy S24 (phone); Call/text records from warrant
I. RYAN McCALL CELLULAR DEVICE EXAMINATION
Ryan McCall's Samsung Galaxy S24 (IMEI: 358742110293847) was examined under warrant. Device was recovered from
McCall at the time of his detention at 534 Conti Street at 3:00 AM on October 4, 2025. Device was in an unlocked state and
powered on. Chain of custody maintained.
Text Messages — October 4, 2025 (Relevant Entries — McCall's device):
Carrier records (obtained via warrant from T-Mobile) were analyzed for Ryan McCall's device. Tower pings are recorded for
each call and data session. Relevant findings:
PING AT 1:28 AM (October 4, 2025): McCall's device connected to Cell Sector ID #NOLA-BRB-04, a T-Mobile cell sector
located on the rooftop of 725 Bourbon Street. This sector's coverage area includes the 700 block of Bourbon Street and the
alley behind it — the crime scene area. The sector does NOT include the Conti Street parking area (two blocks away) where
McCall's car was found. McCall's claimed location ('in my car on Conti Street since 1 AM') is CONTRADICTED by this cell
DEREK FONTENOT CALL TO McCALL AT 1:14 AM: The 1:14 AM incoming call from Derek Fontenot to McCall lasted 1
minute 38 seconds — occurring one minute before the alley camera shows Marcus Webb exiting through the staff door
(1:15 AM), and four minutes before all three suspects exit the same door (1:18 AM). This call is consistent with Fontenot
coordinating with McCall to follow the victim.
III. TEXT MESSAGE ANALYSIS — TRAVIS BELL TO DEREK FONTENOT
The text message from TRAVIS BELL to DEREK FONTENOT at 2:48 AM — 'Don't say anything' — was sent approximately
eight minutes after the PBPD dispatch call was received (2:40 AM) and six minutes before Officer Thibodaux arrived on
scene (2:47 AM). This demonstrates that Bell was aware a body had been discovered and was actively coordinating a
silence strategy with Fontenot. This is consistent with consciousness of guilt.
Sgt. Marcus Chauvin, Digital Forensics Unit Supervisor
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
PBPD DIGITAL FORENSICS UNIT — SURVEILLANCE ANALYSIS
Digital Forensic Examiner Brandon St. Amant / Forensic Analyst Keisha Morel
The Rusty Anchor DVR System (indoor + alley cameras)
Back office, The Rusty Anchor, 712 Bourbon Street
I. INDOOR BAR CAMERA — KEY FINDINGS
The indoor camera covers the main bar area. Footage was intact and complete for the relevant period. Key timestamped
Marcus Webb enters The Rusty Anchor through front entrance. Proceeds to bar.
Derek Fontenot, Travis Bell, and Ryan McCall approach Marcus Webb at bar.
Visible heated altercation — all three men in Webb's personal space. Fontenot pointing.
Bartender (Shannon O'Brien) intervenes. Altercation verbally de-escalates.
Travis Bell visible making gestures toward the back of the bar (back door direction).
Marcus Webb exits through staff/back door.
Derek Fontenot makes call on his phone (confirmed as call to Travis Bell, 1:16 AM).
ALL THREE SUSPECTS — Fontenot, Bell, McCall — exit through back staff door together.
None of the three suspects are seen re-entering the bar after 1:18 AM.
II. ALLEY CAMERA — FOOTAGE GAP ANALYSIS
The alley camera recorded continuously from 12:00 AM until 1:14 AM. The footage shows the empty alley with normal
periodic foot traffic. At 1:14 AM, Marcus Webb is briefly visible approaching the staff door from the alley side (appearing to
use the door from outside briefly, then re-entering — consistent with an earlier visit to the alley or accessing the door). At
1:15 AM the staff door opens and Marcus Webb exits into the alley.
THE FOOTAGE GAP: At 1:15:42 AM, the alley camera footage becomes corrupted — the image goes to digital noise
(multi-colored pixel scatter) and then BLACK. This condition persists until 1:37:18 AM — a gap of exactly 21 minutes and 36
seconds. Upon resumption at 1:37 AM, Marcus Webb is visible on the ground in the alley, not moving.
III. FORENSIC EXAMINATION OF THE FOOTAGE GAP
The DVR system was physically examined and forensically imaged. Examination findings:
1. STORAGE MEDIA EXAMINATION: The hard drive was forensically imaged using an industry-standard write blocker. File
system analysis revealed a DELETION EVENT in the file allocation table corresponding to the 1:15 AM – 1:37 AM period.
The deletion timestamp recorded in the DVR's system log: 1:18 AM, October 4, 2025. The deletion was performed via the
DVR's USER INTERFACE (not a hardware failure) and required physical access to the DVR unit.
2. PARTIAL DATA RECOVERY: Forensic data recovery tools recovered 4 minutes and 22 seconds of fragmented footage
from the deleted period. This recovered footage shows: (a) At approximately 1:18–1:20 AM, three male figures entering the
alley from the staff door direction. Resolution is insufficient for facial identification but body count and attire (one figure in a
light-colored shirt, one in a darker top) is consistent with known suspect descriptions. (b) At approximately 1:22 AM, a brief
fragment shows what appears to be a physical confrontation — multiple figures over a prone figure — but is too degraded
3. EXAMINER CONCLUSION: The 22-minute gap is NOT consistent with a mechanical failure or hardware malfunction.
The deletion event was performed intentionally via user access to the DVR unit. The timing of deletion — occurring at 1:18
AM, precisely when the three suspects entered the alley — is consistent with a deliberate act to destroy evidence.
NOTE: The bar owner (Phillip Arceneaux) has stated the camera 'malfunctions sometimes.' No record of prior malfunction was found in the
DVR event log. The deletion event at 1:18 AM is the only anomalous event in 60 days of system logs.
715 South Broad Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70119
